manifest.name: Input
manifest.description: The Input component provides a flexible and accessible text input field for collecting various types of user data within forms or other interactive interfaces. It supports all standard native HTML input attributes (e.g., `type`, `placeholder`, `name`, `value`, `disabled`, `readOnly`, `required`, `pattern`, `maxLength`). Key features include customizable sizing through 'sm', 'default', and 'lg' variants, allowing for consistent visual styling across different contexts. It's ideal for capturing short textual information like usernames, email addresses, passwords, search terms, or general-purpose text entry where a single line is sufficient. The component integrates seamlessly into forms and ensures proper accessibility by supporting associated labels and ARIA attributes to enhance usability for all users.

valueDescriptions.default: Standard size for general use input fields, balancing prominence and compactness.
valueDescriptions.sm: Smaller size for compact forms, dense layouts, or less prominent input needs.
valueDescriptions.lg: Larger size for prominent input fields, increased readability, or primary data entry areas.

common.title: Disabled With Value
common.code: <Input defaultValue="Cannot edit this" disabled 'aria-label'="Disabled input with value" />

guidance.whenToUse: Use the Input component to collect single-line textual user data such as names, emails, passwords, or search queries. It's suitable when you need a standard text field with custom sizing options and native input attribute support within forms or interactive elements.
guidance.whenNotToUse: Do not use for multi-line text input (use a Textarea instead) or for selecting multiple options (use Checkbox or Radio Group). Avoid using when a specific type of input with dedicated validation or a specialized UI is needed, such as a DatePicker, TimePicker, or a Rich Text Editor.
guidance.accessibility: Ensure associated labels are always present and correctly linked to the input field using `htmlFor` and `id` attributes to provide context for screen readers. Use appropriate `aria-*` attributes for validation, error states, or specific input types (e.g., `aria-invalid`, `aria-describedby`). Provide clear error messages and visual cues for invalid input states.
